question_title: Will the interest in “les wexner” change between 2026-02-17 and 2026-02-22 according to Google Trends?
summary: The search term "les wexner" experienced a massive spike on Feb 10, 2026 (value=100) when Wexner was named as an Epstein co-conspirator in newly unredacted FBI files. Since then, interest has decayed rapidly: 100→78→58→37→27→22→18. On Feb 17, there's an uptick to 25, likely due to anticipation of Wexner's closed-door congressional deposition on Feb 18. The resolution compares Feb 17 (~25) vs Feb 22 values. By Feb 22 (a Sunday, 4 days after the deposition), the post-spike decay pattern strongly suggests the value will be well below 25. Even if the deposition generates a secondary spike, 4 days of decay typically brings values down ~70% from the spike. The only way interest increases is if the deposition reveals dramatic new information sustaining multi-day elevated interest, or if another major news event occurs.

factors.description: Post-spike decay pattern is clear and steep: 100→78→58→37→27→22→18 over 7 days. By Feb 22 (5 more days), decay should continue well below current elevated Feb 17 level.
Feb 17 value (~25) is artificially elevated by deposition anticipation, making it a high starting point that Feb 22 is unlikely to match or exceed.
Feb 22 is a Sunday - weekend search interest is typically lower than weekday interest, reinforcing the decline.
The Feb 18 congressional deposition is closed-door, not a public hearing, limiting its capacity to sustain prolonged search interest.
The deposition could reveal major new information (criminal referrals, explosive testimony) that re-spikes or sustains interest through Feb 22, working against Decreases.
Multiple ongoing legal proceedings (Ohio State lawsuit, Congress) could generate additional news coverage keeping interest elevated.

reasoning: Now let me analyze this data carefully.
